Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a consistent study routine. I'd go through phases where I'd binge-watch lecture videos for hours, then not touch anything for weeks. With 19 online courses going on, I kind of just dealt with whatever was most urgent. My approach was more reactive than planned—I'd study when I felt like it or when something was due soon.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely driven by deadlines rather than any regular schedule. Some weeks I'd barely open my laptop for coursework, other weeks I'd be up late cramming. I have extracurriculars and other things going on, so I fit studying into whatever gaps I had. Regularity just wasn't my thing this semester.
Q3: Usually a deadline notification or an email reminder would get me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I'd missed. Occasionally I'd go in because I was genuinely curious about something from class, but that was rare. Most of the time it was panic-driven, like realizing an assignment was due in two days.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a habit for me—it always felt like a chore. There were brief periods where I'd check it daily, usually right before midterms or finals. But that routine never stuck beyond those crunch times. I just couldn't make myself care enough to log in regularly.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful because I'm a visual learner and I could watch them at my own pace. I'd often speed them up or pause to take screenshots of important slides. The discussion boards were occasionally useful when other students explained concepts differently than the professor. Those felt like the most valuable resources for actually understanding material.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They felt overwhelming and I knew I could get by without them if I focused on the videos and exams. The practice quizzes I'd only do if I had extra time, which was almost never. I also rarely engaged with the weekly reflection prompts—they felt like busywork.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to the exam or final grade, I'd do it. Activities that felt like they'd actually help me understand something tricky were worth my time. But anything that seemed like it was just there to pad out the course or check a box? I'd skip it. I needed to see the payoff clearly.
Q8: Optional activities might as well not exist for me. If it's not required, my brain just files it under 'ignore.' Required activities at least had consequences attached, so I'd force myself through them. Optional stuff felt like extra work when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics. The distinction was basically mandatory versus invisible.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then study from them on my tablet without being connected to the LMS. I also used YouTube videos and other external resources when the course materials weren't clicking. A lot of my actual learning happened with printed notes spread across my bed, completely offline. The LMS was just where I grabbed materials from.
Q10: There were definitely times I'd spend hours working through problems on paper or reviewing my handwritten notes, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd rewatch downloaded videos multiple times while taking notes, but the system only saw that initial download. Some of my best study sessions happened at coffee shops with no wifi, just me and my notes.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then use it repeatedly offline over the following weeks. My downloads folder was basically my real study hub. The LMS would show one access, but I might have reviewed that material ten times. It was just more convenient to have everything saved locally.
Q12: Around midterms, everything felt impossible. I had multiple courses with overlapping deadlines and my stress levels were through the roof. There were weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to engage at all—I'd open the LMS, feel overwhelmed, and close it immediately. The CBT sessions I did helped me recognize that pattern, but it still happened.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or had too many competing responsibilities, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Fatigue made everything feel harder, and confusion about course expectations made me want to avoid it entirely. Sometimes I'd be so tired I'd stare at a video for twenty minutes without absorbing anything. My engagement was directly tied to how overwhelmed I felt.
Q14: Oh, constantly. I'd have videos playing in the background while scrolling my phone, technically 'watching' but not really present. Sometimes I'd click through readings just to mark them complete without actually reading. There were plenty of times I was logged in and active but mentally checked out. The system saw engagement, but my brain was elsewhere.
Q15: I postponed assignments all the time, even important ones. Part of it was anxiety—the bigger the assignment, the more I'd avoid starting it. I'd tell myself I worked better under pressure, but really I was just scared of doing it wrong. Even knowing something was important didn't override that avoidance instinct. It's something I'm still working on.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd barely touch the LMS until something was due within 48 hours, then I'd go into overdrive. My login patterns probably looked like flat lines punctuated by frantic spikes. Without deadlines, I honestly might not have engaged at all. They were stressful but necessary for me.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to try harder on the next thing. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose interest in that aspect of the course. Honestly, I didn't always read feedback carefully—sometimes I'd just check the grade and move on. The feedback that actually changed my behavior was specific and came quickly.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so many modules and resources available. I actually would have engaged more if things were more streamlined and sequential. Having too many options made me freeze up and not choose anything. A clearer, more guided path would have helped me stay on track instead of drifting.
Q19: The flexibility was both good and bad—good because I could work at my own pace, bad because I'd abuse that freedom. Having videos available anytime meant I could always watch them 'later,' and later often never came. Clearer milestones or check-ins might have kept me more accountable. The open structure enabled my worst habits.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—clicking around, downloading things, maybe even watching videos, but not actually producing work. High activity could mean someone is anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. Or they're gathering materials but struggling to actually complete assignments. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That's literally me. I barely logged in but still got a Distinction because I studied effectively offline and crushed the exams. My exam scores were strong because I know how to learn when I actually sit down and focus. The LMS activity just doesn't capture that I'm good at absorbing material quickly when it matters. Low engagement doesn't mean low capability.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and anything that happens offline. They can't tell if you're actually thinking or just going through motions. They show behavior but not learning, presence but not understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking buttons—it's being present and curious. For me, true engagement is rare but powerful when it happens. It's when I forget I'm studying because I'm genuinely interested in figuring something out.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I actually found parts of this course fascinating, even though my attendance was terrible. It wouldn't capture the late-night conversations with friends about course concepts, or how I'd think about the material while doing other things. My engagement was real but invisible—sporadic, offline, and internal. The data tells a story of disengagement, but the truth is more complicated.
